item.title: Progressives want to tax billionaires first, and you're next on the list
item.description: A billionaire wealth tax may sound appealing, but history shows how the income tax expanded from targeting the rich to hitting the middle class.
item.content:encoded:
If you ever wished you could be counted among billionaires, your wish might come true soon if the socialists get their way. Of course, it’s not that you’ll have a billion-dollar net worth, but rather you’ll be subject to the same wealth tax that progressives say they want to foist onto billionaires.

What Russian revolutionary Leon Trotsky said of war applies to the wealth tax: you may not care about it, but it cares about you. Recently, California Democrat Rep. Ro Khanna published his own personal manifesto, "Why I Support a Billionaire Wealth Tax." But he quickly pulled the mask back even further, revealing the true targets of his tax scheme.

Khanna started arguing to tax billionaires, then halfway through the manifesto decided it should be centimillionaires — $100-million net worth. By the end, he was going after for $50-million-aires. At that rate, give it a year or two, and the wealth tax will be $50 thousand, not $50 million.

NEWSOM BEGS CALIFORNIANS TO VOTE 'NO' ON BILLIONAIRE'S TAX IN FACE OF MASS EXODUS, PITCHES NATIONWIDE TAX HIKE

This wouldn’t be the first time progressives pulled this kind of bait and switch — it’s what they did with the income tax. Billionaires are the cheese in the mousetrap, a marketing prop to pass the tax before going after the real target. Once the tax is in place, the communists can get to work knocking zeros off the threshold until it hits pretty much anybody with a job or savings or a home.

This just happened in New York. Comrade Zohran Mamdani, the Democratic Socialist mayor, campaigned on enacting new taxes for billionaires, but he's already pushing tax hikes on millionaires.

Again, this is nothing new. It’s the same play progressives ran with the income tax. It was just a tax on the rich, they promised at the time — for annual incomes equivalent to $15 million today. Back in 1908, Democrat nominee William Jennings Bryan called for taxing "swollen fortunes" of men of large incomes, so that, as the platform explained, "wealth may bear its proportionate share of the burdens of government."

NEW YORK CITY MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI STRUGGLES TO DEFINE 'WORKING CLASS'

Of course, the rich were the bait to get the middle class into the trap. Today, the income tax hits two-thirds of Americans, starting at $15,000, not $15 million. So much for swollen fortunes.

Meanwhile, the tax that started at a rate of 1% to 7% on millionaires now hits the middle class at a 35% marginal rate. Throw in additional taxes on income for Social Security, Medicare and state income tax, and the marginal rate is 50% in places like California.

That’s just the dollars that people lose. It doesn’t include the billions of hours Americans spend in an annual month-long humiliation ritual, financial colonoscopy, under threat of audit and imprisonment.

CALIFORNIA ‘BASIC INCOME’ EXPERIMENT FAILS TO PROVIDE ‘FINANCIAL INDEPENDENCE,’ STUDY FINDS

So, it's nice of progressives to spring the trap early by already removing zeros from the original target of their wealth tax. But even if the tax were limited to billionaires, wealth taxes are catastrophically destructive.

Progressives can’t grasp this because their understanding of billionaires seems to be informed by a cartoon duck who swims around in a vault of gold coins. In reality, billionaires keep nearly all their wealth invested in businesses — Walmart, to Chick-fil-A, Tesla, etc.

So, every dollar taken in a wealth tax isn't scooped out of the gold pool but is liquidated along with the jobs and innovation built by that investment. The companies fade, replaced with generous funding for Somali "Learing" Centers, left-wing NGOs to riot for social justice and starting new and interesting wars.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

And the businesses fade fast — at Khanna's 5% annual wealth tax, you essentially cancel the investment returns. As in, billionaires should just spend the money on yachts and supermodels instead of parking it in jobs and innovation.

Even if progressives grasped this reality, they’d probably keep pushing a billionaire tax because it's one of their few schticks that polls well. Of course, it’s just the first act of a tragedy: Khanna's already pushing for the proceeds to seed a one-year universal basic income that would pay millions to sit on the couch, including illegal aliens.

It’s essentially a venture investment for trillions of dollars in vote-buying bread and circuses forever. The great economist Frédéric Bastiat once said government is the fiction through which everybody endeavors to live at the expense of everybody else. Taxing billionaires — and now millionaires — is the cheese in the mousetrap.

E.J. Antoni, Ph.D., is Chief Economist and the Richard F. Aster fellow at the Heritage Foundation and a senior fellow at Unleash Prosperity.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM E.J. ANTONI

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM PETER ST. ONGE

item.title: SEN MIKE LEE: Trump proves America can stand up to a power-mad foreign court
item.description: Marco Rubio announces the Trump administration will dismantle the ICC through sanctions, visa bans and diplomatic pressure to protect American sovereignty.
item.content:encoded:
At the very core of the American project has always been a fierce defiance of far-off powers, a stubborn opposition to those who would seek to undermine our right to self-rule or impose their will on a people determined to decide their own destiny.

This spirit not only brought America into being but is essential for our nation to preserve its sovereignty into the future.

America again defied those who would undermine our right to self-rule on July 13 when Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced that the Trump administration would "dismantle" the International Criminal Court and teach them "the full meaning of American resolve."

CNN LEGAL ANALYST CALLS OUT 'UTTERLY RIDICULOUS' CLAIM BY MAMDANI ABOUT NETANYAHU ARREST

This will include diplomatic pressure on nations that partner with American law enforcement or our military and enjoy the benefits of our protection, coordination with allies to join us in rejecting International Criminal Court (ICC) authority, sanctions against the ICC and its affiliates, and visa bans for ICC personnel. I have pushed for similar policies legislatively, including the " ICC Out of NYC Act ," legislation that would forbid United Nations properties in New York City from being used, leased or occupied by representatives or employees of the ICC.

The ICC, however, remains convinced of its own power to pierce the veil of American sovereignty and wage lawfare against American citizens, soldiers and public servants alike. A global court that attempts to wield ultimate authority – above and beyond the authority of any nation state – poses a direct and dire threat to the continued sovereignty of the United States that can no longer be tolerated.

As Americans, we wholeheartedly reject the notion that a supranational entity, especially one formed by a statute that was never ratified by our country, has legitimate jurisdiction over our citizens and our leaders. The U.S. Constitution is our law and guide and none other.

To forfeit the legal authority of the United States government to an unaccountable transnational organization would amount to a betrayal of the American people – and an abdication of our right to self-rule.

An active and emboldened ICC means that American sovereignty is contingent on the whims of a globalist entity run by those who have never stood for election in front of the American people, never represented America’s interests, and never sworn loyalty to America’s Constitution. No self-respecting nation, certainly not one founded in rebellion against the tyranny of a far-off power, can allow unelected globalists to wield unchecked power against its citizens, soldiers and leaders.

The United States has worked to impose limits on the ICC for decades, with presidents from both parties warning against the threat posed by the court to American sovereignty. No president, from Bill Clinton to George W. Bush to Barack Obama, has been willing to ratify the Rome Statute, which would surrender American sovereignty into the hands of globalist bureaucrats empowered to launder their political grievances through the language of unenforceable international law.

WORLD COURT PROSECUTOR WHO WENT AFTER NETANYAHU FOR WAR CRIMES SUSPENDED OVER SEXUAL MISCONDUCT

But despite past presidents’ reluctance to fully legitimize the court, none were willing to take decisive action to prevent the severe breaches of sovereignty that the ICC remains capable of committing today. Tepid half-measures are not sufficient in the fight to defend America’s sovereignty.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

Only President Donald Trump is proving bold enough to take decisive action against the capabilities of the ICC, necessary to ensure that American citizens and servicemembers are never subjected to tribunals, and so America’s elected leaders are never threatened with arrest for acting in accordance with our country’s national interest.

No nation is truly independent when it allows an unaccountable court to sit above it and claim jurisdiction over its people and elected officials. Meeker countries may be content handing veto power over their foreign and domestic policy to an unaccountable supranational body, but such a blatant departure from national self-rule is entirely alien to the American tradition, mind and spirit.

It’s why the Trump administration is right to take on the ICC and ensure it never has the capability to threaten the United States and her people.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM SEN. MIKE LEE

item.title: Canada’s wildfire smoke is choking America — Congress must clear the air
item.description: The FIRE Act and FENCES Act aim to reform prescribed burns and protect states from penalties for Canadian wildfire smoke crossing American borders.
item.content:encoded:
This week, millions of Americans woke up to hazy skies and scratchy throats under air quality alerts, not because of anything happening in their own local communities or states, but because smoke from the hundreds of wildfires burning in Canada drifted across the border. Across the Atlantic seaboard to the Midwest, Americans were told to stay indoors. Seniors and people with respiratory conditions faced increased health risks. Kids enjoying their summer break watched their outdoor plans get cancelled. Families on vacation were stuck inside.

America’s own regulatory system allows forests to grow dangerously overgrown, letting dead trees and dense underbrush pile up for years while environmental reviews drag on before a single chainsaw ever starts. Meanwhile, drought intensifies conditions and one lightning strike, or a careless spark, can turn into an inferno. This is the opposite of environmental stewardship and neglect dressed up as caution.

Americans deserve policies that help prevent catastrophic fires before they start, protect our communities when fires do occur, and ensure our environmental laws actually improve the environment instead of burying commonsense solutions in years of bureaucratic delay.

TRUMP BLAMES CANADA FOR WILDFIRE SMOKE, SAYS POLLUTION COSTS SHOULD BE ADDED TO TARIFFS

Under the Clean Air Act (CAA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has established regulations for six critical air pollutants, the majority of which are found in wildfire smoke.

We passed two pieces of legislation on a bipartisan basis to support states in their efforts to mitigate, respond to, and recover from wildfires.

The Fire Improvement and Reforming Exceptional Events (FIRE) Act  rests on a simple truth: preventing catastrophic wildfires is itself an environmental win. Prevention beats response every time, and waiting for disaster to strike is almost always the most expensive, and most dangerous option on the table. The FIRE Act allows states to perform prescribed burns, the most effective tool to mitigate wildfires and their emissions, without being penalized for those mitigation efforts. It also directs the EPA to conduct regional analyses when multiple states are affected by the same wildfire or event, which will reduce regulatory burden and streamline federal action. This bill rewards proactive forest management while protecting public health, and is a commonsense solution to a problem that shouldn’t have existed in the first place.

AIR QUALITY CONCERNS FROM CANADIAN WILDFIRES FORCE RESCHEDULING OF TWO PROFESSIONAL SPORTS GAMES

The Foreign Emissions and Nonattainment Clarification for Economic Stability (FENCES) Act  clarifies that states should not be held accountable for  foreign emissions beyond their control and excludes foreign emissions when the EPA determines whether a state complies with air quality standards to ensure states aren’t penalized for emissions originating outside of the United States.  The FENCES Act protects states, energy producers, and manufacturers from costly and unnecessary compliance requirements. When Canadian wildfire smoke degrades air quality in American communities, it’s an injustice to hold those states accountable for outside pollution and does nothing to solve the underlying cause of the air emission.

The Senate already has what it needs. The FIRE Act clears the way for states to manage their own forests, the best tool we have to address wildfires, without years of bureaucratic sign-off and fear of facing extreme economic sanctions. The FENCES Act makes sure no state foots a federal penalty for emissions it never created. Passing both isn't optional anymore.

Canadian researchers themselves have noted  that wildfire smoke is becoming a recurring feature of Canadian summers. It is simply unacceptable for American communities and businesses to continue being punished and burdened by wildfires raging outside of our own borders. 

POOR AIR QUALITY DUE TO WILDFIRES FORCES LOGISITCAL CHANGES AT SEVERAL MLB GAMES, INCLUDING POSTPONEMENT

Our states know the cost of catastrophic wildfires firsthand. We've watched communities lose homes, businesses, treasured public lands, and, tragically, lives. We've seen tourism suffer, infrastructure damaged, watersheds contaminated, and local economies disrupted. Even after the flames are extinguished, the impacts linger for years through erosion, flooding, degraded wildlife habitat, and diminished water quality. To hold states accountable for pollution originating from another country is just salt in the wound.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE FOX NEWS OPINION

The smoke currently blanketing much of the United States offers a powerful reminder that poor forest management doesn't stay contained within political boundaries. Air pollution doesn't ask whether you're a Democrat or Republican before affecting your family. It doesn't stop at state lines or international borders. It impacts millions of Americans regardless of where the fire started.

The best way to improve air quality across the country is to prevent these catastrophic events from happening, and legislation like the FIRE Act and FENCES Act ensure that a future without these disasters and their lasting negative impact to our environment and communities is possible. That's why these bills matter, and why we’re urging our colleagues in the Senate to pass this legislation quickly. Every day we wait is another day that preventable risks are allowed to grow. We can't control every wildfire, but we can make it easier to reduce their frequency, severity, protect our communities, and restore healthier forests.

We didn't start the fire. We're just done punishing American communities for our neighbors to the north's mess.

Gabe Evans represents Colorado's eight congressional district.

item.title: China’s routing of Biden in chip war with US created a communist tech giant
item.description: Huawei's chip revenue is expected to hit $12 billion in 2026 as China poured billions in grants into the company after U.S. export controls.
item.content:encoded:
Despite American export controls designed to cut Huawei off from the world's most advanced semiconductor technology, China's largest technology company is poised to post a 60% jump in chip revenue this year — a far cry from the death blow the Biden administration had hoped the restrictions would deliver.

Following export controls implemented in 2023, China directed its state agencies to purchase hardware, chips, mobile devices and software from Huawei while also pouring over $1 billion in government grants into the company.

Now, three years later, Huawei is expected to close 2026 with about $12 billion in chip revenue — a massive jump from $7.5 billion in 2025, according to analysts.

The result is fueling debate over whether U.S. export controls have inadvertently made Huawei stronger—or whether they have successfully slowed China's push to compete in advanced semiconductors.

STEPHEN MOORE: GOVERNMENT CONTROL OF CHIP SALES HAS A SHOCKING DOWNSIDE

To some, like Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia, the world’s largest semiconductor engineering company, there’s reason to still believe Huawei should merit respect.

"Huawei is the single most formidable technology company in China," Huang told the Financial Times earlier this year.

"They’ve conquered every market they’ve engaged in," he added.

The U.S. originally placed restrictions on Huawei in 2019, blacklisting its devices from government use.

The Biden administration continued to tighten restrictions in the next few years.

In 2022, it banned sales and imports from the company and, a few months later, halted export licenses to Huawei entirely, citing national security interests. In 2024, the U.S. pressed for many of its allies to follow suit. The Trump administration has continued those efforts in 2025, blocking dozens of Chinese groups from accessing semiconductors and other pieces of technology.

The compounding moves created a de facto ban on the company.

"The export controls announced in the two rules today restrict the PRC’s ability to obtain advanced computing chips, develop and maintain supercomputers, and manufacture advanced semiconductors," the U.S. Department of Commerce wrote in a statement at the time.

"These items and capabilities are used by the [People’s Republic of China] to produce advanced military systems including weapons of mass destruction; improve the speed and accuracy of its military decision-making, planning, and logistics, as well as of its autonomous military systems; and commit human rights abuses."

But by then, China had already begun laying the groundwork for using Huawei as a way to step away from reliance on the U.S.

CHINA IS BUILDING AN AI WAR MACHINE. WASHINGTON MUST WAKE UP BEFORE IT’S TOO LATE

Huawei’s annual reports indicate that the company’s government grants skyrocketed during the same period. From 2.5 billion yuan in 2021, worth roughly $403 million, the company received 6.5 billion yuan in 2022 and then 7.3 billion in 2023.

Notably, government investment dipped in 2025, to 3.9 billion yuan.

Even so, the investments have coincided with an important milestone by 2026: the emergence of the Ascend 910B chip — a jump in China’s cutting-edge technology designed to compete with Nvidia's chips.

The company believes it can go further. In May, Huawei announced that it would adopt new strategies to push the boundaries of existing technology. Instead of trying to make parts inside chips increasingly smaller — a key focus for the rest of the industry — Huawei believes it has found a way to make electrical signals move faster and take more efficient shortcuts, a principle called "Tau scaling law."

He Tingbo, a Huawei executive, seemed to believe it could reverse the company’s isolation.

"No single company can independently find all the answers along the path of semiconductor evolution. With the τ Scaling Law, we look forward to working closely with scientists, engineers, and industry partners around the world to drive the sustainable development of the semiconductor and electronics industries."

While debates remain about just how effective Ascend 910B really is as a challenger and some of the other breakthroughs Huawei has promised, some lawmakers argue that the export controls have succeeded in one key area: forcing China to offshore at least some of its production.

Rep. John Moolenaar, R-Mich., said he believed the move had forced China to rely more on Taiwan.

"Huawei’s only path to meeting China’s internal demand has been to illegally procure chips from Taiwan — a humiliation the CCP would prefer to avoid at all costs," Moolenaar wrote in a letter to Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, referring to a shell company China had used that China had used to get around restrictions.

AMERICA IS FIGHTING YESTERDAY’S AI WAR. TOMORROW’S WAR IS ON THE WAY

"While Huawei struggles to cheat its way toward meeting domestic AI-chip demand, the United States has accumulated roughly 75 percent of the world’s AI computing power."

Representatives for Huawei did not respond to requests for comment from Fox News Digital.

item.title: USA Today columnist who attacked Caitlin Clark has history of injecting far-left views into sports
item.description: USA Today's Nancy Armour, who attacked WNBA superstar Caitlin Clark, has a lengthy history of injecting liberal views into her sports columns.
item.content:encoded:
The columnist for USA Today who sparked outrage over her attacks towards WNBA superstar Caitlin Clark has a lengthy history of injecting her liberal views into sports.

The newspaper's sports writer, Nancy Armour, took aim at the iconic Indiana Fever point guard for repeatedly "flopping" on the court, arguing it's becoming "dangerous" for others, specifically Black players.

She argued the "racists" and "white nationalist faction to Clark’s fandom" will see her as a "white woman in need of saving and take matters into their own hands," citing harassment Black players have faced following various altercations with the basketball star.

"It shouldn’t need reminding this country has an awful history of Black people being harmed, even killed, in the name of 'defending' [W]hite women," Armour wrote Wednesday. "The WNBA's All-Star Game this weekend is in Chicago, Emmett Till's hometown, and a local art gallery is currently hosting an exhibit imagining his life if he hadn't been lynched. While it’s incumbent on the WNBA to do more to protect its players, there also should be an expectation on Clark to recognize the wider consequences of her actions."

DAVE PORTNOY BLASTS USA TODAY COLUMNIST FOR CLAIMING CAITLIN CLARK’S REFEREE COMPLAINTS FUEL RACISM

The writer linking Clark to Carolyn Bryant Donham, the woman who falsely accused 14-year-old Till of making unwanted advances that resulted in his brutal murder, sparked outrage among critics.

Armour walked back what she called an "inartful comparision," insisting she neglected to provide enough context in her column — but doubled down on her argument.

USA TODAY REPORTER APOLOGIZES FOR INVOKING EMMETT TILL IN COLUMN ABOUT CAITLIN CLARK AFTER UPSETTING THE WOKE

"However, this point stands: There is a history in this country of perceived threats to [W]hite women being weaponized against African-Americans, and the danger that poses to Black players in the league," Armour wrote in a statement posted on Instagram. "Players have been abused online and have reported receiving death threats. That has to be taken more seriously."

"I sincerely regret that my lack of appropriate context is overshadowing that important conversation and the action that needs to be taken by the WNBA to address it directly — for the benefit of ALL players," she added.

USA Today added an editor's note to the column saying it was "updated to provide further context and clarity."

Armour, who joined USA Today in 2014, has long fixated on fueling her sports columns with her far-left views.

On Thursday, after her latest attack on Clark, she slammed The Heritage Foundation, who she dubbed the "authors of Project 2025," alleging "they're coming for Title IX" based on a recently-released policy report that concluded the longstanding law that requires schools to provide equal opportunites for men and women has become a "powerful engine of feminist social engineering" that doesn't address the inherent difference between both sexes.

"Our bodies, our self-confidence, our independence — they’re not going to stop until women have the same rights and opportunities they did before the 1970s. Which were almost none," Armour claimed.

"It’s easy to say the Heritage Foundation’s efforts will go nowhere... But Project 2025 has done too much damage already not to take the threat to Title IX seriously," she later wrote. "The Heritage Foundation wants a United States that reflects their White, Judeo-Christian and heteronormative 'values,' and is steadfastly working to eliminate anything that deviates from them... This country has learned the hard way that women can be stripped of what we'd assumed were fundamental rights. Don't let Title IX be the next to go."

SPORTS MEDIA HAS CREATED AN ‘INDUSTRY’ OUT OF COMPLAINING ABOUT WHITE ATHLETES LIKE CAITLIN CLARK

Last month, as the historic FIFA World Cup was underway, Armour declared the United States "lost" due do its "greed and hostility" on the global stage, declaring "we chose to be ugly Americans" as the host nation.

"The U.S. men’s national team could make a historically deep run and the tournament could be filled with wondrous goals and compelling games. It will not change the fact that we have shown ourselves to be a hateful and greedy nation, one where leaders are only willing to welcome a world as they want to see it rather than as it exists, and the love of the game is exploited for top dollar. That will be the legacy, and the shame, of this World Cup," Armour began her column before listing her grievances like the crackdown on Iranian visas as the U.S. continues its war with the Islamic Republic and the skyrocketing ticket prices.

USA TODAY COLUMNIST WHO SLAMMED CAITLIN CLARK DECLARED US GREED WOULD RUIN WORLD CUP

In 2025, Armour rallied behind Olympic gold medalist Simone Biles during her spat with former NCAA swimmer Riley Gaines, who she accused of being "bully" against the transgender community as Gaines became a prominent advocate for women and girls in sports.

"There is no scientific evidence that transgender women athletes have a physical advantage over cisgender women athletes, but that hasn’t stopped Gaines from claiming they do," Armour wrote. "Gaines has publicly lobbied for Biles and Caitlin Clark to support her in her hate which, again, is a laughable idea to anyone who has followed Biles’ illustrious career."

She continued, "Most of us could never do what Biles does as a gymnast. But we can do what she does as a human: Be kind. Defend the most marginalized. And when you get the opportunity to stick up to a bully, do it at full throat. In a world of Riley Gaineses, be a Simone Biles."

SPORTS COLUMNIST FACES BACKLASH OVER SIMONE BILES DEFENSE AFTER RILEY GAINES ATTACK

Armour similarly attacked then-ESPN broadcaster Sam Ponder in 2023 for speaking out in defense of women and girls who object biological male athletes competing in women's sports, who the USA Today columnist accused of "plain old bigotry."

"Don’t be fooled by the people who screech about ‘fairness’ to cloak their bigotry toward transgender girls and women, the transgender girls and women who have the audacity to want to play sports, in particular. This is, and always was, about hate, fear and ignorance," Armour wrote at the time.

In 2021, Armour fumed over how NFL superstar Tom Brady, who was just days away from playing in his tenth Super Bowl, had gotten an "undeserved pass" over his past support for Donald Trump.

"Brady’s ability to enter and exit the debate at his choosing, to shield himself from accountability, is the height of white [sic] privilege," she wrote. As this country grapples with the far reaches of systemic racism, look no further than Brady, for whom the expectations, and allowances granted, will always be different."

Armour wrote a similar column prior to Super Bowl 51 in 2017 that insisted that Brady "has some explaining to do" and "no longer gets a pass on his friendship with Donald Trump."

USA Today parent company Gannett did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

item.title: Witnesses recount 'scary' NYC stabbing after suspect allegedly yelled 'Allahu Akbar' during attacks
item.description: A suspect allegedly yelled "Allahu Akbar" before stabbing two men on Manhattan's Upper West Side, with NYPD investigating mental health as a factor.
item.content:encoded:
Witnesses recounted the moments surrounding a pair of New York City stabbings being investigated as a possible hate crime, with one calling the scene the "scariest thing" she had ever seen.

The suspect, identified as Raul Morales, 51, is accused of yelling "Allahu Akbar" while stabbing two men on Manhattan’s Upper West Side on Thursday afternoon, according to officials.

The NYPD is investigating whether bias played a role in the attacks. One victim is Jewish, while the other is Asian, police said.

Police said the stabbings appeared to be separate attacks that occurred near each other. Authorities said there was no known connection between Morales and either victim or between the two victims.

SUSPECT ARRESTED AFTER ALLEGEDLY YELLING ‘ALLAHU AKBAR,' STABBING 2 MEN IN NYC ATTACK

Both men were taken to a hospital in stable condition.

Authorities are investigating whether a knife or screwdriver was used in the attacks.

A witness described seeing a knife sticking out of one of the victims following the stabbing.

"I just saw him sitting on his knees not moving at all, with this knife sticking out. It was the scariest thing I've ever seen. The men who worked in the building are amazing and they just ran to get help," the witness told Fox 5 New York.

Another woman, who said she was friends with one of the victims, said she was inside a synagogue when she heard that someone had been stabbed. When she reached the scene, the victim was already being taken to the hospital.

"I spoke to direct eyewitnesses who said that this man had yelled 'Allahu Akbar' and had taken a screwdriver and stabbed him directly in the chest ... then the guy walked away and kind of pretended he was not culpable and said 'maybe someone should call 911, it looks like someone's hurt on the street,'" Nava Silton told the outlet.

Silton said she later visited the victim in the hospital and described him as brave and courageous.

"It is incredibly scary and he's just praying the condition doesn't get worse. He had to pull the screwdriver out of his body," she said.

Initial NYPD findings suggest mental health may have been a factor.

"While the perpetrator has no known mental health history with the NYPD, the initial investigation suggests that mental health may have been a factor," Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch said.

AMERICAN TOURISTS STABBED OUTSIDE ATHENS' ACROPOLIS AS POLICE DETAIN SUSPECT IN APPARENT RANDOM ATTACK

Officials across the state denounced the attack, including New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani and Gov. Kathy Hochul.

"These hateful and despicable attacks have no place in our city," Mamdani said.

Hochul said she was "disgusted by what appears to be another senseless attack on New Yorkers simply because they’re Jewish."

Anyone who tries to tear that apart will be met with the full force of the law. New York will stand with our Jewish community today, tomorrow, and always," she said.

item.title: Multiple injured after floatplane makes emergency landing, catches fire off Washington coast
item.description: A Kenmore Air seaplane carrying 11 people made an emergency landing near Sucia Island on the Washington coast before catching fire, with one person in critical condition.
item.content:encoded:
Multiple people were injured Thursday after a Kenmore Air floatplane made an emergency landing off the Washington coast before catching fire, prompting a massive rescue operation, authorities said.

The U.S. Coast Guard said it received a report around 5:25 p.m. local time that a Kenmore Air seaplane had made an emergency landing in North Beach Shallow Bay off Sucia Island before bursting into flames.

Authorities said all 10 passengers and the pilot were accounted for.

SEN TIM SHEEHY MAKES EMERGENCY LANDING AFTER IN-FLIGHT ENGINE FAILURE

The San Juan County Sheriff's Office said multiple people suffered injuries ranging from head trauma to broken bones and lacerations.

Kenmore Air said four passengers were taken to local hospitals for treatment. Authorities said one person was in critical condition. No fatalities were immediately reported.

The airline said it was working closely with investigators and would provide additional updates as more information becomes available.

MISSOURI SKYDIVING PLANE CRASH THAT KILLED ALL 12 ABOARD IS A 'DEVASTATING LOSS,' COMPANY SAYS

Kenmore Air CEO David Gudgel said the company's immediate focus was on those aboard the aircraft.

"We are grateful that everyone aboard has been accounted for, and our immediate focus is ensuring our passengers and pilot receive the care and support they need," Gudgel said.

"We extend our sincere thanks to the United States Coast Guard, local emergency responders, and everyone assisting in the response," he continued. "Kenmore Air is fully cooperating with the investigating authorities, and we will continue providing verified information as it becomes available."

The airline said the aircraft involved was a de Havilland DHC-3 Otter operating near Sucia Island.

According to the sheriff's office, the flight departed Seattle's Lake Union and was headed to Roche Harbor Marina on San Juan Island.

A Kenmore Air spokesperson told Fox News Digital the route between Lake Union and the San Juan Islands is a routine flight for the airline.

"If you live here, you would be potentially taking a seaplane up there," the spokesperson said.

COAST GUARD HELICOPTER CRASHES DURING ALASKA TRAINING MISSION, INJURING FOUR CREW MEMBERS

The sheriff's office said multiple agencies responded by deploying boats and emergency personnel to the scene.

The U.S. Navy also responded, while three air ambulance helicopters transported injured passengers.

Orcas Island Fire & Rescue, Lopez Island Fire & Rescue, San Juan Island Fire & Rescue and San Juan County Parks also responded.

The cause of the accident remains under investigation.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the U.S. Coast Guard for additional comment.

item.title: Two found shot to death inside car on University of Chicago campus: report
item.description: Two people were found with gunshot wounds to the head inside a vehicle near Midway Plaisance Ice Skating Rink on the University of Chicago campus.
item.content:encoded:
Two people were shot and killed inside a vehicle on the University of Chicago campus on Thursday, according to local reports.

The victims — a 27-year-old woman and a 24-year-old man — were discovered inside a vehicle on the 5900 block of South Ellis near Midway Plaisance Ice Skating Rink and Harper Quad at around 5:35 p.m., police said, per NBC 5 Chicago.

Both victims had a gunshot wound to the head, according to police.

MANHUNT UNDERWAY AFTER COLLEGE STUDENT FATALLY SHOT CHASING ROBBERS WHO ALLEGEDLY STOLE HIS PHONE

The man and woman were transported to the university hospital in critical condition, but were later pronounced dead.

It remains unclear if the two victims had any connection to the university.

CHICAGO'S DEADLY JUNETEENTH WEEKEND LEAVES 7 DEAD AS TRUMP SHAMES DEM GOV FOR INACTION

A firearm was recovered from the scene, police said.

Video of the scene showed that the vehicle, which was damaged, appeared to have crashed either during or after the shooting, NBC 5 Chicago reported.

The incident remains under investigation.

"This incident is currently under investigation, and we cannot confirm details at this time," a Chicago Police Department spokesperson told Fox News Digital.

Fox News Digital reached out to university police for additional information.

item.title: Former Fever player responds to Sophie Cunningham by alleging Indiana harassment cover-up
item.description: Brianna Turner claims the Indiana Fever fired a male staff member for sexual harassment, challenging Sophie Cunningham's stance on trans athletes.
item.content:encoded:
The Indiana Fever are facing new scrutiny after former player Brianna Turner accused the organization of firing a male staff member over sexual harassment allegations during her time with the team, weighing in after Sophie Cunningham's comments on trans athletes.

Turner, a Fever player in 2025 and now with the Las Vegas Aces, made the allegations on X after Cunningham defended women's sports for biological females during an ESPN interview.

Cunningham said she wants to "protect young girls in a locker room, or young girls in sport who shouldn't have to go against biological men."

WHITE HOUSE DEFENDS WNBA STAR SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM AFTER SHE SPEAKS OUT IN SUPPORT OF PROTECTING WOMEN'S SPORTS

Turner barked at Cunningham's claims, saying organizations like the Fever are a greater threat to the game than trans athletes. According to Turner, the Fever fired a male staff member after allegations of sexual harassment but instructed players not to discuss the matter publicly.

She madly defended trans inclusion, pointing to former WNBA player Layshia Clarendon, who was born female before later identifying as transgender.

"To date there's been one out trans player in the league. They caused zero problems," Turner wrote on X.

WNBA PLAYER FACES BACKLASH OVER OP-ED CRITICIZING IOC FOR POLICY TO PROTECT WOMEN'S SPORTS

Turner contrasted that with the alleged conduct of the fired staff member, adding, "That is an issue. Sexism, misogyny, homophobia, and racism are all issues."

The Indiana Fever released a statement Thursday in response to Turner's claims.

"Protecting our players and employees and maintaining a respectful, professional workplace is our top priority," the Fever said.

"Our policy is to take concerns seriously, investigate thoroughly, and act promptly when appropriate to protect the people in our organization and ensure everyone is treated with professionalism and respect."

The team concluded, "Fostering trust and safety in that process means we do not comment on personnel matters involving former employees."

It remains unclear whether the WNBA will review Turner's allegations.

As of Thursday, the league had not announced an investigation or publicly commented.

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

item.title: California mayor's hot mic catches candid exchange after audience sends meeting off rails
item.description: A hot mic caught Sacramento Vice Mayor Karina Talamantes and Mayor Kevin McCarty discussing whether to ban disruptive protesters from future meetings.
item.content:encoded:
A live microphone exposed a behind-the-scenes conversation between Sacramento's mayor and vice mayor as they discussed whether disruptive protesters could be barred from future city council meetings after a heated immigration-related debate descended into chaos.

"We should go over this disruptions thing, because if we want to ban somebody in the future, like to take them out, we have to point out specifically…" Vice Mayor Karina Talamantes is heard saying.

"I know, but the city attorney said we can't do that," Mayor Kevin McCarty replied.

"We did it last time," Talamantes responded.

GOP SENATORS RALLY AROUND NEW DHS PROPOSAL TARGETING SANCTUARY CITIES: 'SHOULD PAY A PRICE'

"We can't," McCarty said, shaking his head. "I spent my first six months going over this. I wanted to give people, like, probation, like, you can't come…"

Talamantes then appeared to realize the microphone was still live and quickly reached over to switch it off before continuing the conversation, according to the publicly available city council livestream reviewed by Fox News Digital.

The awkward exchange unfolded Tuesday after Sacramento City Council recessed and ordered the public removed from the chambers following repeated disruptions during a fiery debate over the city's continued participation in a regional intelligence-sharing center.

The confrontation escalated after Talamantes defended accepting federal funding for the Central California Intelligence Center, or CCIC, arguing Sacramento's sanctuary city policies and immigrant protections made the city better suited to oversee the grant than other jurisdictions.

"The city of Sacramento is a sanctuary city," Talamantes said during the meeting. "If we don't pass these dollars, other counties will apply for these dollars, they will get those dollars and we will have no safeguards to protect our immigrant communities here in Sacramento."

Dozens of speakers had earlier urged the council to reject the agreement, arguing the intelligence center could facilitate information sharing with federal immigration authorities and undermine Sacramento's sanctuary city policies.

Police officials repeatedly disputed those claims during the meeting, telling council members Sacramento does not share personal identifying information for civil immigration enforcement and complies with California's sanctuary law, known as SB 54. 

DHS LAWYER SAYS UCLA 'UTTERLY FAILED' TO STOP PROTEST CHAOS AT LAW SCHOOL APPEARANCE

As public comment grew increasingly heated, one speaker repeatedly demanded McCarty pay attention.

"Kevin, I need your f---ing eyes. Kevin, listen to me. Stop being an a--hole, dude. Listen to your f---ing constituents," the speaker shouted.

Following Talamantes' remarks, another round of shouting erupted inside the council chambers.

WATCH: HEARING DERAILS AS PURPLE-HAIRED DEM POINTS FINGER, SCREAMS AT CHAIR TO PUT DHS CHIEF 'IN HIS PLACE'

"You're a pick-me b----," one attendee shouted at Talamantes, while others yelled, "Shame on you."

McCarty responded with what he described as a "final notice."

"This is the final notice," the mayor said. "We're going to continue with the deliberation, and if there's any more outbursts, we're gonna have to ask the audience to leave."

Seconds later, the disruptions intensified.

"You can't do this behind closed f---ing doors!" one attendee shouted.

"We are the community," another yelled.

"You represent us. You are our f---ing audience," another voice exclaimed as council members attempted to continue the meeting.

Council officials then paused the meeting and cleared the chambers after repeated disruptions prevented deliberations from continuing.

Following the meeting, McCarty said the private conversation focused on existing meeting procedures, not new restrictions.

"Every Tuesday, Vice Mayor Talamantes and I are responsible for overseeing and ensuring transparent, accessible, and open Council meetings," McCarty said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"Unfortunately, when meetings are repeatedly disrupted, we must discuss existing policies and rules to ensure Council meetings remain effective, orderly, and respectful for both the residents in attendance and the Councilmembers. We are committed to ensuring that all members of the public can access and engage safely without distraction or intimidation."

The Sacramento City Attorney's Office declined to address McCarty's reference to legal advice during the hot-mic exchange.

"The City Attorney's Office has no comment and will not be providing comment on this matter," the office said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

The city's published meeting rules state that members of the public receive two minutes per agenda item and may speak on no more than four items during a meeting, limits intended to help the council conduct city business.

The hot-mic exchange quickly drew criticism from some activists, who argued Talamantes appeared interested in silencing vocal opponents.

Joshua Masias, a volunteer organizer with the Sac Immigration Committee, told The Sacramento Bee the conversation showed an authoritative approach toward members of the public.

"It's very authoritative how she wants to use her power," Masias told the outlet. "It stuck out to me that she was the only one to try to silence the crowd."

Resident Leanna Hunt, one of the council's most outspoken critics, also defended the protesters' frustration.

"I'm fed up," Hunt told the Bee. "It's not even a tactic — I'm just tired of being treated like we are an audience and that the king is deigning to see us peasants today and when the peasants are unruly, the king gets upset."

Fox News Digital reached out to Vice Mayor Talamantes' office, Sacramento Police Department and the Sac Immigration Committee for additional comment.

item.title: ESPN's Adam Schefter weighs in on LeBron James' NBA future after Heat press conference post sparks buzz
item.description: Adam Schefter revealed a text suggesting LeBron James is heading to Miami, while a deleted Heat YouTube post listed an introductory press conference.
item.content:encoded:
LeBron James made it clear last month that retirement was not yet in his plans. The four-time NBA MVP said he plans to keep playing in 2026-27, but not with the Los Angeles Lakers.

Sixteen years ago, James placed his free agency at the center of a nationally televised spectacle. "The Decision," a special tied to his work with the Boys & Girls Clubs of America, ended with James revealing he would leave the Cleveland Cavaliers for the Miami Heat.

James left Miami in 2014 to return to his home state of Ohio, where he ultimately delivered the Cavaliers their first NBA title in 2016. He changed teams again in 2018, joining the Lakers.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

More recently, speculation has surfaced that James could be eyeing another return, this time to South Florida. Miami only fueled that talk earlier this week when it shared a since-deleted social media post linking to what was billed as the "LeBron James Introductory Press Conference."

A Heat team spokesperson later told ESPN the purported livestream event was not legitimate.

"I haven't read anything about, I haven't spoken to (anyone about it). But my guess would be where there's smoke, there's fire," ESPN NFL insider Adam Schefter said in about the link in question.

AGENT RICH PAUL SAYS LEBRON JAMES WON'T BE RUSHED ON NBA FREE AGENCY DECISION DESPITE OUTSIDE PRESSURE

Schefter’s comments aligned with the growing speculation that James could land in Miami alongside Giannis Antetokounmpo and Bam Adebayo next season.

"Had a text that said just be ready for LeBron to go to Miami. I can read you the exact text," Schefter continued during a recent appearance on Philadelphia's 97.5 The Fanatic. "‘I’m hearing Bron is coming to the Miami Heat, just want to give you a heads up. You may want to check it out.’"

Schefter pointed to a tip he said he received on June 30, when reports first surfaced that James would consider playing his 24th NBA season away from the Lakers.

The Cavaliers, Golden State Warriors and Philadelphia 76ers have also been linked to James.

James remained productive last season, averaging 20.9 points, 7.2 assists, and 6.1 rebounds across 60 games. He remains the NBA’s all-time leading scorer.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: Tomi Lahren defends OutKick's ESPN scoop, torches Jemele Hill, backs Sophie Cunningham
item.description: Tomi Lahren fired back at Jemele Hill over OutKick's ESPN scoop and praised Sophie Cunningham for defending women's sports from trans athletes.
item.content:encoded:
Jemele Hill came after OutKick over our scoop on ESPN host Ryan Clark's departure.

Tomi Lahren answered with a blowtorch.

On Thursday, Lahren appeared on both "Tomi Lahren Is Fearless" and "America Reports." Across the two appearances, she addressed two of the week's biggest sports stories: Jemele Hill's outrage over OutKick's reporting and Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham doubling down Wednesday on her support for protecting women's sports from trans athletes after the WNBA media predictably lost (her) mind.

WHITE HOUSE DEFENDS WNBA STAR SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM AFTER SHE SPEAKS OUT IN SUPPORT OF PROTECTING WOMEN'S SPORTS

Lahren started with Cunningham, praising the Fever guard for refusing to retreat from what she called a matter of common sense:

Jemele Hill criticized OutKick after we broke the news of ESPN's latest layoffs before the network publicly announced them. Journalism, we're told, had finally gone too far.

EXCLUSIVE: ESPN TO PART WAYS WITH CAM NEWTON AS PART OF TALENT LAYOFFS THAT ALSO INCLUDED RYAN CLARK

Hill called for OutKick's demise and said she'd need to be rushed to the hospital from laughter alone.

So, Lahren unloaded on Jemele.

WATCH:

OutKick founder Clay Travis summed up Tomi's response: "off the top rope!"

Even Stephen A. Smith has taken positions on women's sports that would've been unthinkable at ESPN just a few years ago.

Hill is still defending a media playbook her former industry keeps throwing away.

Meanwhile, Cunningham isn't backing down. Neither is OutKick.

Tomi Lahren hosts "Tomi Lahren Is Fearless" on OutKick.com — where she delivers her signature unapologetic takes on the biggest stories in culture, sports, and politics, plus interviews with influential voices from entertainment, social media, and more.

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

item.title: Massachusetts Democratic Party chairman admits to sufficient facts in June drunken driving case
item.description: Steve Kerrigan's breathalyzer registered 0.089 after a Southborough traffic stop, just above state's 0.08% legal limit, court records show.
item.content:encoded:
The leader of the Massachusetts Democratic Party was arrested during a traffic stop on alleged drunken driving charges last month and days later admitted to prosecutors there were sufficient facts to support a conviction, according to state court records.

A judge on June 15 — two days after the traffic stop in the town of Southborough — continued the operating under the influence (OUI) liquor charge without a finding after Steve Kerrigan, chairman of the state's Democratic Party, entered an "admission to sufficient facts," a Massachusetts legal disposition in which a defendant acknowledges prosecutors have enough evidence to obtain a conviction, according to Westborough District Court records.

In addition to the OUI charge, Kerrigan, 54, faced a negligent operation charge and a marked lanes violation. The negligent operation charge was dismissed, and he was found not responsible for the marked lanes violation.

The judge sentenced Kerrigan to a year's probation, suspended his driver's license for 45 days, ordered him to pay $600 in fees and required him to complete an impaired driving education program.

COLORADO DEMOCRATS FORMALLY CENSURE GOV POLIS OVER TINA PETERS COMMUTATION

Kerrigan was elected chair of the Massachusetts Democratic Party in 2023. He was the Democratic nominee for lieutenant governor in 2014, and previously served as an executive with the Democratic National Convention Committee. He also serves as president and CEO of the Edward M. Kennedy Community Health Center.

According to a Southborough Police Department report, an officer stopped Kerrigan shortly after midnight on June 13, after observing his BMW traveling on or over the lane divider.

The officer wrote that Kerrigan's eyes appeared "glassy" and "bloodshot red" and that his speech was slow and slurred. The report states the officer could not detect the smell of alcohol because of what was described as an "overwhelming odor of cologne."

FLORIDA MAN ARRESTED FOR DUI AFTER TROOPERS ALLEGEDLY FIND 34 OPEN ALCOHOL CONTAINERS IN VEHICLE

Kerrigan told the officer he was driving home from a restaurant and said he had consumed two beers around 6 p.m. before dinner, according to the report.

Police administered field sobriety tests before Kerrigan provided a breath sample that registered a 0.089% blood alcohol content, according to the report. The legal blood alcohol limit in Massachusetts is 0.08%.

Kerrigan acknowledged the incident in a statement provided to Boston.com through his attorney, Andrea Levy.

"In June, I was pulled over for a marked lanes violation and blew a .089 on the breathalyzer," Kerrigan said. "I regret and take full responsibility for my actions and their consequences and appreciate the Southborough Police Department and the Westborough Court for their professionalism and service."

The Massachusetts Democratic Party did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

item.title: Rubio unveils visa restrictions targeting cybercrime networks
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced sweeping new visa restrictions targeting individuals complicit in cybercrime, online investment scams and sextortion.
item.content:encoded:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced on Thursday a new visa restriction policy targeting foreign nationals responsible for or complicit in cybercrime and cyber-enabled crime.

"This policy targets individuals responsible for, or complicit in, cybercrime and cyber-enabled crime, such as those involved in cyberscams, and sextortion," Rubio said in a statement.

Certain immediate family members of people covered by the policy may also be subject to the State Department's new visa restrictions, according to Rubio.

TRUMP LAUNCHES GOLD EAGLE TO HUNT CYBER FLAWS WITH AI

President Donald Trump signed an executive order earlier this year titled "Combatting Cybercrime, Fraud, and Predatory Schemes Against American Citizens" directs federal agencies to develop measures against foreign scam centers, transnational criminal organizations and cyber-enabled financial fraud.

The administration, Rubio said, "is taking action to counter an unprecedented threat from online investment scams."

WHAT A SCAMMER SEES THE MOMENT THEY GOOGLE YOUR NAME

"Often orchestrated by Chinese transnational criminal organizations, scammers have defrauded U.S. citizens of at least $10 billion in 2024 alone while fueling corruption, money laundering, and human trafficking," the secretary said.

"American children also have been specifically targeted by offenders operating overseas for sextortion scams, devastating families and futures," he continued.

Rubio said the administration is "deploying every tool at our disposal—sanctions, prosecutions, asset seizures, extradition requests, and international law enforcement cooperation—to dismantle criminal scam networks and impose costs on those who enable them."

"By restricting visa issuance to those who are responsible for or complicit in these criminal enterprises, we are sending a clear message: The United States will go after those who prey on our citizens," he said.

item.title: Trump DOJ withdraws subpoenas targeting New York Times journalists amid leak probe
item.description: The Department of Justice withdrew its subpoenas of New York Times journalists involved in the report about President Donald Trump's use of the Qatar jet.
item.content:encoded:
The Justice Department withdrew subpoenas Thursday it had previously filed against three New York Times journalists as part of a leak probe.

The DOJ sought the grand jury testimony and phone records of Times journalists Tyler Pager, Julian Barnes, Eric Schmitt and Eric Lipton, all of whom had a byline on a report published earlier this month revealing President Donald Trump flew home from Turkey on his old Air Force One instead of the new Qatar-donated Boeing 747-8 he flew into the NATO summit amid security concerns, something Trump denied.

The Times report, citing anonymous sources, prompted a leak probe by the DOJ, hoping to target those who gave the Times journalists the sensitive information.

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION SUBPOENAS NY TIMES JOURNALISTS IN GRAND JURY LEAK PROBE TIED TO AIR FORCE ONE REPORT

"Today’s proceeding was an important affirmation of our country’s commitment to a free press," New York Times Senior Vice President and Deputy General Counsel David McCraw said in a statement to Fox News Digital. "We are pleased that the government finally conceded that the subpoenas violated the law, but they should never have been issued in the first place. The Times and our journalists will continue to report and seek the truth without fear or favor. We will not be deterred in the face of tactics like these."

The Justice Department did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

HEGSETH ANNOUNCES JOINT TASK FORCE WITH DOJ TO PROSECUTE LEAKS TO JOURNALISTS 'WITH THE FULL FORCE OF THE LAW'

Upon his departure from the NATO summit in Turkey, Trump flew out on his old Air Force One instead of the new one gifted to the administration by Qatar that he had previously used. However, the Times alleged the change was made due to the escalated military tensions between the U.S. and Iran.

TRUMP SAYS HE'S IRAN'S 'NO. 1' TARGET AS RENEWED CONFLICT RAISES ASSASSINATION FEARS

Trump dismissed any security concerns with the jet, instead posting on Truth Social that the Mildenhall stop was conducted so service members stationed on the base could view the new aircraft.

The subpoenas targeting the Times journalists sparked an outcry among critics, accusing the Trump administration of violating First Amendment rights.

The DOJ previously defended the subpoenas, highlighting the crime of leaking national security information while stressing that the Times journalists weren't the target of the probe.

"We value and appreciate the important role that the press plays in this country, but DOJ also plays an important role to make sure that the people entrusted with our nation's secrets do what they're supposed to do with that information, which means not sharing classified information," a DOJ spokesperson said at the time. "We recognize there may always be natural tension there, but we are not going to ignore the law and stop investigating the people who work in the administration and think it's okay to leak classified information impacting national security."

Fox News' Julia Bonavita contributed to this report.

item.title: Scottie Scheffler addresses growing rivalry between Rory McIlroy, Bryson DeChambeau after The Open
item.description: Scottie Scheffler shared his thoughts on the Bryson DeChambeau and Rory McIlroy rivalry ahead of the 3M Open, saying golf is different from other sports.
item.content:encoded:
The Open Championship just closed out the 2026 major championship season, and somehow one of the most unexpected winners in recent memory, Ryan Fox, hasn't been the story.

Instead? It's once again Bryson DeChambeau. DeChambeau was a lightning rod for criticism ahead of The Open when analysts Nick Faldo and Brandel Chamblee had harsh words for his approach. Faldo said he lacked the "strategy" to succeed in links golf, that he hits driver as far as he can and hopes for the best instead of trying to "thread" it down the fairway.

Chamblee had a broader complaint: He believes DeChambeau has spent more time chasing YouTube golf star Grant Horvat than trying to run down the best in the world, Rory McIlroy and Scottie Scheffler. So how did Bryson respond? In the most Bryson way possible.

BRYSON DECHAMBEAU CLAPS BACK AT NICK FALDO, OTHER CRITICS, AFTER -3 ROUND AT THE OPEN: 'INCREDIBLY STRATEGIC'

He immediately put himself in contention with a strong 3-under 67 first round. Afterward, he said in his media appearance that he had been very "strategic" when playing Royal Birkdale, a clear response to Faldo. Round two, however, was where he really became the center of attention.

After the round was completed, a seeming 4-under 66, rules officials brought DeChambeau back to the fifth hole, where they assessed him a two-stroke penalty for improving the area around his backswing. DeChambeau was furious, and apparently threatened to withdraw from the tournament in protest.

Some players, like Xander Schauffele, came to his defense. Scottie Scheffler also was adamant that the 32-year-old is not a "cheater."

One player who didn't come to his defense? Rory McIlroy. McIlroy was clear in his post-round comments that he doesn't care for DeChambeau and believed he deserved the penalty.

"I won’t pretend to be up here and defend Bryson," he told reporters during the tournament. "I’m not particularly fond of him. I think a lot of it is performative. I think a lot of it’s for attention.

"To hold the tournament hostage like that and to have all of us — players, volunteers, everyone — waiting on him to depart, I didn’t feel like it was a great look," McIlroy said.

BRYSON DECHAMBEAU DETAILS THE TWO COMPLICATED HURDLES IN HIS WAY OF A POTENTIAL PGA TOUR RETURN

"I think there’s no doubt that he improved the line of his backswing," he added. "Again, it’s like, whether it was careless or whether it was intentional, I don’t think it matters. Hopefully, it was careless, but I think the two-shot penalty was justified, for sure."

This marked the latest entry in the budding rivalry between the two big names and big personalities in professional golf. They're on opposite sides of Ryder Cup battles. DeChambeau was one of the earliest and largest stars to depart for LIV Golf, while McIlroy became a sort of player spokesman defending the PGA Tour. McIlroy has never done a full 18-hole match on YouTube, while DeChambeau's made his channel into a behemoth, filming rounds with President Donald Trump and many others.

Ahead of this week's 3M Open at TPC Twin Cities in Minnesota, Scheffler was asked whether he believes the rivalry is good for golf, and gave a typically incisive answer.

"I think golf's different in a sense of a lot of other sports because my biggest rival week in and week out is the golf course that we're playing," he told the media. "I mean, I could never stop somebody from doing anything in golf. I felt like when I played basketball there would always be rivalries in basketball because you're going to play defense against a person and that is much more personal in a sense like because I'm actively trying to stop...like if you and I were going to go out and play one-on-one, it would turn into a rivalry because I'm actively trying to stop what you're doing.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"But in golf, we're always competing against ourselves on the golf course. I've never been a big fan of personal jabs. I don't think that rivalry is a big thing in golf just because, like I said, my biggest rivalry week in and week out is the golf course."

There have been a few instances of McIlroy and DeChambeau facing off with a tournament on the line, though they've never gone head-to-head in a Ryder Cup. Most of the rivalry seems to be McIlroy disliking Bryson for either his personality or his departure for LIV, and DeChambeau playing into it, because as he's put it, "rivalries are good" for golf.

Which is what makes the rivalry so fascinating. It's not competitive, it's personal. Scheffler and McIlroy have faced off in Ryder Cups, for example, yet there seems to be only mutual respect between the two.

One thing is certain, though: 2027 will be a fascinating season in the golf world. Between the potential collapse of LIV Golf, the (mostly) iconic venues hosting major championships and yet another Ryder Cup where both are guaranteed selections, this time back in Europe, there'll be plenty of opportunities for Rory and Bryson to face off.

item.title: Timothy Olyphant revealed the surprising truth behind his computer-free lifestyle
item.description: Timothy Olyphant revealed his surprisingly low-tech lifestyle on the "SmartLess" podcast, admitting he had to dig out an old desktop computer to try to connect.
item.content:encoded:
Call it an old-school approach.

Timothy Olyphant revealed he had to resurrect an old computer in an attempt to log on to the "SmartLess" podcast. Ultimately, he wound up using a friend's office to make the interview happen.

"I needed a computer," Olyphant, 58, told hosts Jason Bateman, Will Arnett and Sean Hayes. "I guess you can't do your show if you don't own a computer."

As the hosts pressed him, Olyphant doubled down on his surprisingly low-tech lifestyle.

JENNIFER GARNER KEEPS HER KIDS OFF SOCIAL MEDIA: 'FIND SCIENTIFIC EVIDENCE' IT'S GOOD FOR TEENS

"I got like a, you know, I got a phone and I got a thing," the "Justified" star said. "That's all you gotta do."

"I don't buy it," Bateman questioned. "You don't have, and you being an artist, you don't have an iPad with one of those little, one of those little stylist pen thingies?"

The "Five-Star Weekend" actor then explained that while he technically owned a desktop computer, it had been sitting unused long enough to become a project in itself.

"We went… I had a… we went and got out an old desktop computer that we still have in the house and plugged it in and started it up, and they were all USB things."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

The aging machine proved to be another obstacle. After digging out the outdated desktop and getting it running, Olyphant joked that appearing on the podcast required far more effort than expected.

"There's a lot of sacrifices that went into this guys," he admitted. "And don't get me wrong, I appreciate ... I'm super honored, and it is a pleasure to be here. I'm a big fan. I listen – I don't listen – but I read the transcripts. And the show's very good. Very, very, very good."

Bateman wasn't ready to let the conversation go, continuing to question Olyphant's aversion to computers.

"But wait, but Tim, you've never been tempted to buy a laptop or a desktop just to see the landscape out there, like all the different sites you can go visit to expose yourself to?" Bateman said.

"Felt condescending," Olyphant replied.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Bateman then shifted the conversation to artificial intelligence, wondering whether Olyphant had any interest in the technology despite avoiding computers.

"All this talk about AI and stuff. Not curious to see what the fuss is about?" the "Arrested Development" actor added.

"But you can do all that on… on the little phones," Olyphant emphasized.

Olyphant landed his breakthrough role in 1999 with the cult crime comedy "Go." His performances in "Deadwood" and "Justified" cemented his star status. His other notable works include "Santa Clarita Diet," "The Mandalorian," "The Book of Boba Fett" and "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood."

item.title: Megan Rapinoe blames Trump for US Mens soccer team losing 'underdog' vibe in World Cup
item.description: Megan Rapinoe said President Donald Trump's involvement in the World Cup killed the U.S. men's team's underdog vibe and threw them off before the Belgium loss.
item.content:encoded:
Megan Rapinoe put blame on President Donald Trump for the U.S. mens soccer team's World Cup loss on Tuesday, saying the president's involvement killed the team's underdog vibe in the tournament.

"The Daily Show" host Desi Lydic asked Rapinoe about the red card given to U.S. player Folarin Balogun in their Round of 32 match, which was eventually suspended. Trump admitted to calling FIFA President Gianni Infantino to ask for a review of the red card.

"How do you think that affected the men's team?" Lydic asked Rapinoe.

"Certainly not well, it didn't give them the boost, maybe, that they were thinking it would or that Trump was hoping that it would," she responded. "I don't know. It seemed like it kind of like, threw them off their game."

FOLARIN BALOGUN ADMITS THAT RED-CARD REVERSAL AFFECTED USA WORLD CUP TEAMMATES: 'A LOT OF OUTSIDE NOISE'

"But it didn't go well. They had, like, such a good vibe," she added. "I feel like they had, like, the underdog kind of vibe going. And then you-know-who got involved, and everyone was like, nope. No. We don't like it. And then, obviously, Belgium was like, 'No, we absolutely can't lose to this team now.'" 

Rapinoe, who won two World Cup titles with the women's national soccer team, said she didn't know the rules and wasn't a referee.

"No other president in history could have pulled off the historic feat that President Trump delivered for the American people and the world. Thanks to his bold vision and decisive leadership, the 2026 FIFA World Cup generated billions of dollars in revenue, created a massive economic boost across host cities, and delivered a safe, secure, and seamless experience for millions of fans and athletes from around the globe. It will undoubtedly go down as one of the greatest and most successful sporting events in history," White House spokesman Davis Ingle told Fox News Digital in a statement.

Rapinoe said during her podcast "A Touch More: The Beautiful Game," that Trump's involvement was a distraction.

LA GALAXY COACH GREG VANNEY SAYS TEAM USA WAS 'TOO NAIVE,' EXPLAINS CHRISTIAN PULISIC'S STRUGGLES

"I think the distraction got to the team for sure in some type of way. Whether it was, you know, this red card situation, whether it was being in a public discourse with this president, all of the, you know, shenanigans and chaos that comes with the feelings on both sides of that. Whether you agree with it or not it's — it's just like it's a lot to contend with," Rapinoe said.

"So, not only does this team not have like a core of older players who are used to that, but none of these players talk about anything ever, like for the most part," she continued.

"I think in men's football, just like in general, there's not a lot of players who are choosing to use their platform or to, you know, broaden their scope of pressure and responsibility," she said.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

The U.S. men's national team lost to Belgium in the Round of 16 match following the red card controversy.

Balogun told CBS after the loss that there was a lot of outside noise leading up to it.

"My initial reaction was I was happy to be back in the team, but when I kind of started to reflect, I knew it was going to cause a lot of controversy, and I could almost see within my teammates a bit of nerves, because it is something that is so unique," Balogun told CBS in mid-July.

"The closer we got to the game, I tried to just focus as best as I could, but it was difficult. A lot of outside noise and that’s hard to avoid," he added.

item.title: Ex-Denver Nuggets star Ty Lawson hurls 'white b---h' insult at cop after Tito's vodka theft arrest
item.description: Ty Lawson allegedly stole a $34 bottle of Tito's vodka while intoxicated, then repeatedly hurled slurs at a Castle Rock Police officer during his arrest.
item.content:encoded:
Ty Lawson made millions of dollars during an eight-year NBA career. Last week, a $34 bottle of Tito's vodka led to his arrest.

Instead of showing remorse, the former Denver Nuggets guard went on the attack, repeatedly calling a Castle Rock Police Department officer a "white b*tch" during his arrest before later claiming on social media that he was the victim.

The bottle of vodka was worth $34. Lawson was booked into Douglas County Jail on a shoplifting charge.

JAMES HARDEN ARRESTED IN HOUSTON ON MISDEMEANOR WEAPONS CHARGE AFTER NBA PLAYOFF EXIT: REPORT

According to the arrest details, Lawson smelled strongly of alcohol during his arrest on July 16. He allegedly took the bottle of Tito's while intoxicated and was later found by officers sitting at a nearby restaurant.

The retired NBA player then took to social media to defend his behavior, instead launching into a familiar "America is racist" rant on X.

According to the arrest report, Lawson repeatedly taunted the officer by calling her a "b—h" and "white b—h" during the encounter.

ANGEL REESE’S LATEST WNBA CONTROVERSY SHOWS HOW VICTIMHOOD BECAME CENTRAL TO HER BRAND

He later wrote on X: "Being black in America is tough…. Being lied to by a pasty face white woman wild… stay outta castle rock Colorado!!!!

"This the wildest and more racist s–t I been thru."

For someone insisting he was the victim of racism, Lawson showed little hesitation in reducing the arresting officer to the color of her skin.

SHAQUILLE O'NEAL SAYS HE DOESN'T TEXT NBA PLAYERS PAST OR PRESENT BECAUSE 'THEY'RE A--HOLES'

From 2009 to 2015, Lawson was a key piece of the Denver Nuggets' backcourt. He also played on Roy Williams' NCAA championship-winning North Carolina team in 2009.

Many online questioned how a former NBA player who earned more than $40 million could respond to the incident by casting himself as the victim instead of taking responsibility.

One X user responded, "It's racist to call out someone who made 40 mill and stole a bottle? Such a pathetic victim mentality."

Lawson also spent time alongside Chauncey Billups in Denver. Billups is currently under federal scrutiny amid allegations involving illegal high-stakes poker games.

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

item.title: Dem’s stunning ideal standard for immigration detention conditions resurfaces amid Texas senate bid
item.description: James Talarico's resurfaced 2019 comments on immigration detention conditions raise questions as he prepares to face Ken Paxton for Texas Senate.
item.content:encoded:
During a 2019 interview following a visit to border enforcement, James Talarico, candidate for U.S. Senate in Texas, said he gauged the suitability of immigration detention facilities based on how he would treat his own family.

"I think I was struck by the conditions these folks were being held in," Talarico said.

"When I first walked in, I looked at one of the security monitors and saw these things strewn around the floor, and I realized they were human beings being held in these tiny rooms. The standard I use is: would I allow my child, my parent, my grandparent to be held in one of these facilities? And the answer, after my visit, was a resounding no," Talarico said.

His resurfaced comments continue to raise questions about his views on border policy as he looks to flip a Republican-held Senate seat in Texas.

PROGRESSIVE TALARICO KNIFES BIDEN'S OPEN BORDER, TRIES MODERATING STANCE ON KEY ISSUES IN TEXAS SENATE RACE

During his campaign, Talarico has drawn scrutiny for walking a tightrope between traditionally Democratic criticisms on President Donald Trump’s border enforcement measures and appearing too soft on border policy.

Like progressives in the party, Talarico has called on lawmakers to "tear down" Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), has characterized voter ID requirements as a method to rig elections and, in the past, even criticized President Barack Obama’s record on immigration.

In one 2019 remark, Talarico famously called Obama the "deporter-in-chief."

His past comments have left some observers believing his record makes the case for him.

"There is a very concerning pattern with James Talarico siding with pedophiles, criminal illegal aliens and cartels while slandering the brave men and women of law enforcement who keep Texans safe," Zach Kraft, a spokesperson for the Republican National Committee (RNC), said in a statement, referring to detentions highlighted by the Department of Homeland Security.

ICE ARRESTS 15 ILLEGAL IMMIGRANTS WITH CONVICTIONS INCLUDING HOMICIDE, RAPE AND CHILD SEX CRIMES OVER WEEKEND

"Talarico is a true radical and fully committed to defunding the police, knocking down President Trump's border wall and welcoming millions of illegal aliens into America," Kraft added.

At the same time, Talarico has voiced concern about the government’s effective use of resources on the border and whether its investments are being reflected in its detention operations. 

He raised the issue in the 2019 interview.

"How are we spending close to a billion dollars every year on border security when these border patrol agents told us firsthand they didn’t have the resources, basic logistical items that would help the situation better for them and for the people being held?" Talarico said.

Talarico argued that poor standards don’t speak to individual facility needs but rather reflect broader problems with immigration enforcement.

"This is not a matter of some bad apples at a Border Patrol station. This is a systemic problem that even the Border Patrol agents identified as something morally wrong and something that needed to be solved," Talarico said in an interview.

"I believe this is a statewide problem; this is a stain on all of our reputations. It violates a lot of the values we have as Texans."

Texas spent roughly $800 million on border security in 2019, according to the state’s legislative budget board.

TALARICO MAKES STUNNING CLAIM ABOUT WHY HE THINKS TEXAS ELECTIONS AREN'T 'FREE AND FAIR'

When pressed for more details about Talarico’s ideal detainment conditions a reality, his campaign pointed to his border security plan he recently unveiled and noted that he has also raised concerns about border security ineffectiveness under the Biden administration.

"James supports stronger border security and safe, secure, and humane detention facilities. He’s the only candidate in this race to unveil a border security agenda endorsed by Republicans and Democrats that will add more border patrol agents and improve technology at the border to better detect threats. For too long, both parties have failed our border communities by grandstanding on the issue instead of finding real solutions. James is running for Senate to keep our country safe by deporting criminals instead of locking up moms, babies and small business owners who have been contributing to our economy for decades," a campaign spokesperson said.

Having secured the Democratic nomination, Talarico will face Republican nominee Ken Paxton in the Nov. 3 general election.

item.title: Carly Pearce says she doesn't want children and calls her first marriage a 'start-up marriage'
item.description: Carly Pearce revealed on TikTok that she doesn't want children but "absolutely" wants to remarry after her divorce from singer Michael Ray.
item.content:encoded:
Country star Carly Pearce has revealed that kids are off the table.

Earlier this week, Pearce responded to a fan on TikTok who asked her if she was still deciding on children and if she would ever remarry. Pearce was married to singer Michael Ray for a year before they divorced in 2020.

"Maybe some of you out here are like me. I always feel bad saying that I actually don't want children. I love children, I love my friends' children, I'm a great aunt. But, it was never my dream," the 36-year-old said.

Pearce went on to explain that her dream was always to pursue country music and live on the road.

COUNTRY SINGER RAELYNN FILES FOR DIVORCE FROM ARMY VETERAN HUSBAND AFTER DECADE-LONG MARRIAGE

"I think we should normalize people that truly know they don't wanna have kids. I don't think it should be for everyone just because society tells us to," Pearce stated. "So, I don't want children and I'm totally okay with that."

Pearce addressed the fan's second question about marriage and said she "absolutely" wants to get married again. The "truck on fire" singer said that she would never let her past sway her away from marriage.
